name: Hazard Pointers
summary: Maged Michael's wait-free read-protection scheme (2002/2004): each thread publishes single-writer/multi-reader pointers naming the objects it currently dereferences; retirers scan all hazard pointers before freeing. Bounded retired-list size O(N x H x R), integrated ABA fix without DCAS. Production form: Folly hazptr; standardised as C++26 std::hazard_pointer (P2530R3).

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Maged Michael's **hazard pointers** (2002 / 2004) provide safe memory reclamation for lock-free data structures by having each thread publish single-writer/multi-reader pointers naming the objects it is currently dereferencing. Retirers scan all hazard pointers before freeing — if any thread has a hazard pointer pointing at the candidate, reclamation is deferred until that pointer is cleared.

Provides wait-free read protection using only single-word atomic stores plus a release/acquire fence (or seq_cst) per acquire. Bounded retired-list size O(N x H x R) (threads x HPs per thread x retire batch), giving worst-case bounded retention — the canonical strength of hazard pointers and the property that distinguishes them from epoch-based schemes. Integrated ABA fix without DCAS.

sections.title: Cost model and trade-offs
sections.content:
Cost: ~1 store + 1 fence (often a full `mfence` on x86) per protected read; retire is amortised O(H x N) scan. The per-read fence is the main overhead — heavier than EBR's pin (relaxed/acquire) but with the compensating advantage that retention is bounded regardless of thread stalls.

The contrast with EBR is sharp: EBR has cheaper readers but unbounded retention if a thread is preempted while pinned; hazard pointers have heavier readers but rigorous worst-case bounds on retired-list growth. This trade-off animates the entire SMR design space — most later schemes (`data/hazard-eras`, `data/interval-based-reclamation`, `data/hyaline-reclamation`, `data/wait-free-eras`, `data/vbr-reclamation`, `data/nbr-reclamation`) are attempts to combine HP-style bounds with EBR-style throughput.

sections.title: Production and C++26 standardisation
sections.content:
Production implementations: Folly `hazptr` (Facebook, in production since 2017), OpenBSD/Concurrency Kit, McKenney's RCU "hazard variant." The reference implementation behind C++26 standardisation is Folly's `hazptr`.

**C++26 `std::hazard_pointer`** (P2530R3) lifts hazard pointers into the language standard alongside RCU, providing thread-cached `hazard_pointer` objects, `hazard_pointer_obj_base<T>::retire()`, and multi-domain support — essentially a portable subset of Folly's API.
